data.title: Hassett Says Trade Data Highlights Onshoring of Production — Market Talk
data.description: National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett says onshoring of production because of tariffs was evident in yesterday's Commerce Department report that showed the U.S. trade deficit shrink to its lowest level since 2009. "A lot of it was capital goods, and so all of this onshoring of production because of the tariffs was really, really visible, not only in the domestic data for capital goods," Hassett says on CNBC. "Everything is looking like the economy is ramping up at a really, really high rate," he added. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; @jessica_coacci)

data.title: How a Clothing Brand in Maine Got a Boost From Maduro's Arrest — WSJ
data.description:
By Ashley Wong

When Pete Roberts saw photos of ousted Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro being led by Drug Enforcement Administration agents across the tarmac at New York's Stewart International Airport, he thought he was looking at a doctored image. On the chest of the strongman's blue hoodie appeared to be the distinctive wave logo of Roberts's Maine-based apparel company, Origin.

"I was like, 'Oh, you're funny, this is AI or something,'" Roberts recalled telling a former employee who texted the photos to him.

But upon closer inspection, Roberts was convinced that Maduro was wearing one of Origin's made-in-America designs: the RTX hoodie in Patriot Blue.

What happens when the controversial leader of a foreign country is extradited in a historic arrest on federal drug trafficking charges — and he's wearing the clothing you made? Concerned about the possibility of backlash, Roberts consulted a business partner, who advised him to seize the moment. His brand had never been on such a prominent figure before, notorious or otherwise.

"'He is wearing an Origin hoodie, that's out of your control,'" Roberts recalled him saying. "You just need to make it about the product and the brand, and do not politicize it."

On Sunday, Roberts posted a five-minute video on social media reacting to the news, then explaining his company's ethos: to bring manufacturing jobs back to America and use all-American sourcing.

"If you're interested in wearing the fabric of freedom, like America's DEA agents and Special Forces, pick up a few other things too, like Delta jeans and some gray boots," Roberts said in the video. "Just placed my order!" John Shahidi, whose media company Shots manages popular podcasts like Theo Von's "This Past Weekend," wrote on X in response.

Roberts says that after he posted on social media claiming the hoodie as Origin's, people started asking to buy it. He then decided to open a new preorder for the style. So far, they have sold at least 1,000 units in Patriot Blue. During a 24-hour period beginning Sunday, sales at Origin were 300% higher than average, Roberts said, and website traffic was 400% higher than average. Both returned to baseline on Monday.

A fake review on the Origin website written by someone pretending to be Maduro gave the hoodie five stars, calling it "excelent (sic)." "It's great to hear that the construction and performance are hitting the mark, " the company responded. Roberts, who was initially unaware that the company had responded, said the website's replies to customer reviews are usually automated and AI-generated.

A Defense Department spokesperson declined to comment. The DEA did not respond to requests for comment.

In the hours and days after his capture, Maduro was seen in multiple outfits that drew attention on social media and became fodder for memes.

Shortly after Donald Trump posted a photo of Maduro wearing what appeared to be a gray Nike sweatsuit, social media lit up with jokes about Maduro's "drip" — internet slang referring to good outfits or accessories. A spokesperson for Nike declined to comment.

Internet users also compared a brown jacket he wore while being taken to a Manhattan courthouse — likely to be standard-issue clothing given to inmates — to those made by Prada or Carhartt.

This kind of commentary can open the door for people to participate in culture and make sense of complicated geopolitical events in an accessible way, said David Schmid, associate professor of English and pop culture expert at the University at Buffalo.

"You're hovering on the edge all the time between, 'Is that kind of remark just trivializing a really, really serious situation, or is it in its own way a kind of political commentary on the absurdity of this whole situation?'" said Schmid.

In an era where images travel fast, the clothing choices of controversial figures can have knock-on effects for businesses. When Luigi Mangione, the man charged with killing UnitedHealth executive Brian Thompson, was photographed during a December 2024 court appearance wearing a burgundy sweater, eagle-eyed internet users identified a similar sweater made by Nordstrom. One day later, the posters noticed the sweater was sold out.

Thomas Matthew Crooks, the man who attempted to assassinate Donald Trump at a 2024 campaign rally, was photographed shortly before the shooting wearing a shirt made by the popular YouTube channel Demolition Ranch, a now-defunct channel known for firearms tests and stunts. In response, the channel's host, Matt Carriker, released a video expressing deep shock and condemning Crooks.

Roberts still doesn't know how the hoodie possibly ended up on Maduro's back, though his personal theory is that federal agents put him in the brightly colored shirt to keep him visible in a dark environment. Of his decision to post about the incident, he said, "I thought it was a good opportunity to shed light on American-made goods."

Write to Ashley Wong at ashley.wong@wsj.com

data.title: Guardian Metal Resources PLC Announces Director's Dealing

LONDON, UK / ACCESS Newswire / January 9, 2026 / Guardian Metal Resources plc (LON:GMET)(OTCQX:GMTLF), a strategic mineral exploration and development company focused on Nevada, US, announces that the Company has been informed that Ben Hodges (Finance Director) has purchased on-market 17,501 Guardian Metal shares at a price per share of 142.99p, for an investment value of approximately £25,024.

About Guardian Metal Resources

Guardian Metal Resources PLC (LON:GMET)(OTCQX:GMTLF) is a strategic mineral exploration company driving the revival of U.S. mined tungsten production and strengthening America's defense metal independence. The Company is advancing two co-flagship tungsten projects, Pilot Mountain, one of the largest undeveloped tungsten deposits in the U.S. and Tempiute, formerly America's largest producing tungsten operation, both located in Nevada, one of the top-rated mining jurisdictions in the U.S.

In July 2025, the U.S. Department of War (DoW) under Title III of the Defense Production Act of 1950, as amended, invested US$6.2M in Golden Metal Resources (USA) LLC, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Guardian Metal Resources PLC, to support the Pilot Mountain PFS. The Company has announced plans to pursue a U.S. listing in the first half of 2026.

Tungsten is a strategic metal critical to the defense, energy transition, technology, and industrial sectors. In the context of shifting geopolitical dynamics and tightening Chinese export restrictions, Guardian is well positioned to play a leading role in re-establishing a secure, domestically mined U.S. supply chain for this vital defense metal.

data.title: Ekinops Appoints Lionel Chmilewsky as Chief Executive Officer
data.description:
PARIS, Jan. 9, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — The Board of Directors of EKINOPS (Euronext Paris - FR0011466069 - EKI), a leading supplier of optical transport, branch routing and cybersecurity SASE solutions for telecom operators and enterprises, today announces the appointment of Lionel Chmilewsky as Chief Executive Officer (CEO), effective January 12, 2026.

Lionel Chmilewsky brings extensive international experience in technology, telecommunications and cybersecurity, notably in the United Kingdom and the United States, and is recognized for leading strategic growth initiatives and complex transformation plans as well as building and managing high-performing multinational teams.

Prior to joining Ekinops, Lionel Chmilewsky notably served as President and CEO of TrustBuilder, a leading European SaaS cybersecurity company, specializing in multi-factor authentication and identity & access management. Previously, as CEO of Corero Network Security plc., an anti-DDoS cybersecurity leader listed on London Stock Exchange, he doubled revenue and ARR([1],) delivered sustained profitability and significantly accelerated growth in the US market. Lionel Chmilewsky also served as CEO of Cambridge Broadband Networks and Comverse IP Communications. He also serves as Chairman of Hive Streaming.

Under Lionel Chmilewsky's leadership, Ekinops will continue to implement its "Bridge" strategic plan, focusing on innovation, customer satisfaction, expansion into high-growth markets as well as introducing new drivers for growth and sustainable value creation.

"The Board is pleased to welcome Lionel Chmilewsky as Ekinops' new Chief Executive Officer. Lionel brings deep technology and leadership experience, particularly in telecommunications and cybersecurity. His mission will be to continue driving international business development and to further strengthen the Group's direct and indirect sales organizations under the Bridge strategic plan, reinforcing Ekinops' positioning in data center interconnection (DCI) for Optical transport and cybersecurity (SASE([2]) ) for Access" said Hugues Lepic, Chairman of the Board. "We would also like to thank Philippe Moulin for his commitment and successful leadership during the transition period. Philippe will carry on as Chief Operating officer."

"I am deeply honored to join Ekinops as Chief Executive Officer and grateful for the trust placed in me by the Board of Directors" stated Lionel Chmilewsky, incoming CEO of Ekinops. "I look forward to putting my expertise to work alongside Ekinops' teams and contributing to the Group's future success in high-growth markets. Ekinops is a recognized leader in its technological fields, with strong teams and an impressive customer base around the world."

"We are fortunate to welcome Lionel Chmilewsky, whose expertise and track record are major assets for Ekinops. The executive team is fully engaged and looks forward to continuing the execution of the Bridge strategic plan and strengthening the company's growth drivers under his leadership. Over recent months, we have prepared in detail our FY 2026 plan, and I would like to thank the Board of Directors for its confidence during this transition period," added Philippe Moulin, Chief Operating Officer of Ekinops.

data.title: Build NYC to Sell $634.2 Million of Bonds for Bronx Independent Living Facility
data.description:
By Zaeem Shoaib

Build NYC Resource Corp. plans to sell $634.2 million of bonds that will help fund an independent living facility for a continuing care retirement community in New York.

The issuance consists of $310.2 million of Series 2026 A Bonds, $65 million of Series 2026 B-1 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities and $87 million of Series 2026 B-2 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS.

In addition, the sale includes $161 million of Series 2026 B-3 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities and $11 million of Series 2026 C Revenue Bonds.

All other securities being issued are tax-exempt, except the Series 2026C bonds.

Interest on the bonds will be payable on June 15 and Dec. 15.

Build NYC Resource is a not-for-profit local development corporation.

Proceeds of the bonds will be loaned to RiverSpring Health Senior Living for financing the construction, renovation, furnishing, and equipping of an approximately 441,000 square foot independent living facility for a continuing care retirement community in the Bronx, New York.

RiverSpring, a New York not-for-profit corporation created to develop and operate a continuing care retirement community, will also use issuance proceeds for the demolition of an existing building located on an adjacent leased parcel of land and the construction of certain improvements

The community operator will also use the funds for the re-payment of pre-construction financing for development costs and repayment of funds advanced by an affiliate for pre-construction and development costs.

The bonds are not rated.

B.C. Ziegler & Co. and Herbert J. Sims & Co. are lead managers for the issuance.

Write to Zaeem Shoaib at zaeem.shoaib@wsj.com

data.title: FTSE Climbs to New Record
data.description:
The FTSE 100 rose 0.8% to a new record of 10128 on Friday, snapping a two-day losing streak as strength in energy, defence and mining stocks lifted the index back into positive territory.

Gains were led by the mining sector after reports of talks between Glencore and Rio Tinto over a potential mega-deal sparked merger-and-acquisition optimism.

Glencore shares jumped around 8.5%, hitting their highest level since July 2024, while other miners such as Antofagasta and Anglo American also advanced as deal speculation spread across the sector.

In contrast, Rio Tinto shares fell about 3%.

Oil majors Shell and BP rose 3.3% and 2.6%, respectively, as crude prices climbed for a second straight session, while defence stocks, including Rolls-Royce and BAE Systems, gained over 1.5% amid continued support for the sector.

Meanwhile, healthcare stocks lagged, and retailers underperformed after Sainsbury’s shares fell more than 5% following a subdued festive trading update.

data.title: Brazilian Real Appreciates to 1-Month Highs
data.description:
The Brazilian real strengthened past 5.37 per US dollar to over one month highs as the latest domestic price and labor data reinforced the central bank’s restrictive stance, outweighing persistent US dollar strength.

Headline inflation slowed to 4.26% in December from 4.46% in November, the lowest since August 2024 and slightly below expectations, while core measures continued to signal firm services price pressures that justify maintaining tight monetary conditions.

At the same time, record low unemployment underscored labor market resilience despite elevated real borrowing costs, supporting domestic demand and policy credibility.

With the Selic rate held at 15% and 10 year yields still in double digits, Brazil remains one of the most attractive carry destinations among emerging markets, even as the US dollar stayed supported by resilient US activity and reduced expectations for near term Fed easing.

data.title: Adobe's Competitive Moat Looks Less Secure
data.description:
Adobe is running into a harsher reality check on Wall Street. BMO Capital Markets downgraded the stock to Market Perform from Outperform, saying the shares are likely to stay range bound as competition intensifies and near term catalysts are hard to spot.

BMO also lowered its price target to $375 from $400. The firm said Adobe's valuation is not the issue. The bigger problem is rising pressure in the creative software market. In a note led by analyst Keith Bachman, BMO said Adobe now ranks at the bottom of its front office coverage, while it prefers rivals like Salesforce and HubSpot.

Survey data is driving the concern. BMO found that more than 50% of students now use Canva instead of Adobe, while nearly half of freelancers rely on Canva compared with about 10% using Adobe alone. More than half of users said they use both tools, a shift BMO views as unfavorable given Adobe's historic dominance.

With Canva widely expected to pursue an IPO in 2026 or 2027, competitive pressure may only increase. Adobe shares were down about 2% Friday and are roughly 20% lower over the past year, trailing the broader software sector.

data.title: AI Is Mining Our Trash for Treasure — WSJ
data.description:
By Conor Grant

This is an edition of The Future of Everything newsletter, a look at how innovation and technology are transforming the way we live, work and play. If you're not subscribed, sign up here .

Here's a role the computers can take without much complaint: sorting recyclables.

It's a difficult job that pays workers little and is hard for companies to fill. Thanks to recent advancements, some recyclers are now employing machines to do this dirty work.

This week, Ryan Dezember reports on the recycling companies using AI to find valuable commodities in our trash.

Murphy Road Recycling's material recovery facility in Berlin, Conn., uses a largely unmanned system of conveyors, magnets, optical sorters and pneumatic blocks. Overseeing all these components are computers, which analyze material as it passes by at about 7 miles an hour.

The devices, made by London-based Greyparrot, use AI to identify recyclables, flag food-grade material, gauge items' mass, assess market value and calculate points at which a robotic claw might best clasp each piece. Then, the precisely sorted material is sold to mills, manufacturers and remelt facilities, which pay more for cleaner bales.

How much Waste Management, the largest U.S. trash hauler and recycler, is spending on building and automating recycling facilities

Spurred on by environmental concerns — and the White House's push to boost domestic production of raw materials — other companies across the U.S. are also building automated systems in a push to more efficiently extract commodities from America's waste.

More on this topic:

  • Trash pickup's future could feature self-driving bins and quieter trucks. (Read)
  • 🎥 This is why investors love garbage. (Watch)

🤔 Do you think AI will change how America manages its waste? Send me your thoughts, questions and predictions at future@wsj.com (if you're reading this in your inbox, you can just hit reply).

More of What's Next: Hospitals Embrace AI; Microbots; Offices of the Future

Hospitals are a proving ground for what AI can and can't do. Healthcare systems are using the tech for everything from reading patient scans to fighting insurance denials, testing AI's utility and revealing — sometimes via alarming mishaps — where it falls flat.

Scientists created robots smaller than a grain of sand. Researchers at the University of Pennsylvania and the University of Michigan made the world's smallest, fully programmable, autonomous robots, packing significant capacities into a tiny device.

WSJ readers shared what they want to see in workplaces of the future. Their ideas for improving office life ranged from creating "no-device speakeasy" areas to adding more workplace fitness options such as underdesk treadmills.

Nicole Nguyen tested AI fitness coaches who wouldn't accept her excuses. After trying out bots from Fitbit, Peloton and Apple, the WSJ columnist learned that the best kind of coach — AI or otherwise — is one who will watch and listen.

Future Feedback

Last week, we reported on police departments that are using remotely controlled drones for law enforcement. Readers shared their thoughts on the use of drones by police:

  • "Drones absolutely have a legitimate part to play in certain aspects of day-to-day policing activities. However, utilizing drones for speed enforcement clearly crosses the line and smacks of 'Big Brother.' The public will not stand for it. Plus, the optics stink from a public relations standpoint!" -- Daniel Garfield, Ohio
  • "Most police forces are chronically understaffed, and crime -- certainly in my area -- is rising. I think we have no choice but to accept more technology-powered policing, both now and in the future. I'm OK with it."
    -- Patrick Brockett, California
  • "Given the current culture of law enforcement, this just doubles down on not simply being masked but also on being masked by remote operation. At least normal aircraft such as helicopters can be identified visually. Drones? Not so much. I think a landowner would be within their rights to remove noncompliant drones from their airspace. Skeet, anyone?" -- Bryan Pike, Massachusetts

(Responses have been condensed and edited.)

Elsewhere in the Future

  • This is Europe's drone-filled vision for the future of war. (MIT Technology Review)
  • Meet the scientists trying to save wine from wildfires. (The New Yorker)
  • Disney wants you to add yourself to your favorite Marvel movie with AI. (The Guardian)

About Us

Thanks for reading The Future of Everything. We cover the innovation and tech transforming the way we live, work and play. This newsletter was written by Conor Grant. Get in touch with us at future@wsj.com. Got a tip for us? Here's how to submit.

See more from The Future of Everything at wsj.com/future-of-everything.

data.title: EU member states confirm approval of signing EU-Mercosur trade agreement
data.description:
European Union member states confirmed on Friday a broad majority of them support a planned free trade agreement with South American bloc Mercosur, Cyprus, which holds the EU rotating presidency, said.

EU capitals had had until 5 p.m. Brussels time (1600 GMT) to confirm their votes in writing after ambassadors of the EU's 27 members signalled their provisional approval of the deal a few hours earlier.

The deal is set to be the largest trade accord the EU has concluded, although it will still require approval from the European Parliament before it can enter force.

data.title: U.S. Consumer Sentiment Improves Slightly More Than Expected In January
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A report released by the University of Michigan on Friday showed consumer sentiment in the U.S. has improved by slightly more than expected in the month of January.

The University of Michigan said its consumer sentiment index rose to 54.0 in January after climbing to 52.9 in December. Economists had expected the index to inch up to 53.5.

With the bigger than expected increase, the consumer sentiment index reached its highest level since hitting 55.1 in September.

'Improvements in January were seen among lower-income consumers, while sentiment fell for those with higher incomes,' said Surveys of Consumers Director Joanne Hsu.

'All told, while consumers perceived some modest improvement in the economy over the past two months, their sentiment remains nearly 25% below last January's reading,' she added. 'They continue to be focused primarily on kitchen table issues, like high prices and softening labor markets.'

The report said the current economic conditions index climbed to 52.4 in January from 50.4 in December, while the index of consumer expectations crept up to 55.0 in January from 54.6 in December.

On the inflation front, the University of Michigan said year-ahead inflation expectations held steady at 4.2 percent in January.

Hsu noted the year-ahead inflation reading remains at its lowest level since January 2025 but is still well above that month's 3.3 percent.

Meanwhile, the report said long-run inflation expectations rose to 3.4 percent in January from 3.2 percent in December.

'In comparison, readings ranged between 2.8 and 3.2% in 2024, and were below 2.8% throughout 2019 and 2020,' said Hsu.

data.title: Dollar Could Stay Steady For Now, More Likely to Fall Than Rise — Market Talk
data.description: The dollar is likely to remain stuck in a range for now, although the U.S. currency remains at risk of falling, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. Given that both volatility and investor conviction are low, range-bound trading is likely until clearer trends emerge, they say. That said, there is still scope for the dollar to fall if investors see greater prospects of interest-rate cuts or if they start to price in a greater risk premium into the currency. "The risks are skewed toward a lower not a higher dollar, particularly against risk-sensitive currencies," the analysts say. The DXY dollar index is last up 0.2% at 99.152. It remains stuck in a range between 96 and 100, Morgan Stanley says. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)
